reference: GATE CS — Databases / Constraints and Normalization

map.condition: Counting all supersets of a key (superkeys) when key size is k in n-attribute relation
Deciding if a set of attributes is a candidate key
map.use: K \text{ is a candidate key} \iff K^+ = R \text{ and } \nexists K' \subsetneq K : K'^+ = R

map.condition: Checking referential integrity: can an INSERT violate it?
map.use: \text{INSERT into referencing } R_1 \text{ may violate; INSERT into referenced } R_2 \text{ never violates}

map.condition: Cascade delete: tuple deleted from referenced table
map.use: \text{DELETE from } R_2 \Rightarrow \text{all tuples in } R_1 \text{ with matching FK also deleted}

map.condition: Foreign key value in referencing table must exist in referenced table
map.use: \forall t_1 \in r_1,\; t_1[FK] \neq \text{NULL} \Rightarrow \exists t_2 \in r_2 : t_2[PK] = t_1[FK]

formulas.name: Superkey Definition
formulas.latex: K \text{ is a superkey of } R \iff K^+ = R
formulas.plain: K is a superkey if the closure of K includes all attributes of R
formulas.whenToUse: Determining whether a set of attributes can uniquely identify every tuple

terms.means: set of attributes
attribute closure of K under the given FDs
set of all attributes in the relation

formulas.trap: Any superset of a superkey is also a superkey; GATE often tests whether you inflate the count by confusing superkeys with candidate keys

formulas.name: Candidate Key Definition
formulas.latex: K \text{ is a candidate key} \iff K^+ = R \;\wedge\; \nexists K' \subsetneq K : K'^{+} = R
formulas.plain: K is a candidate key if K is a superkey and no proper subset of K is also a superkey (minimality)
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying minimal sets of attributes that uniquely identify tuples

terms.means: any proper subset of K
closure of the proper subset

formulas.trap: Distinct values in a column in ONE instance only proves it CAN be a key for that instance, not that it IS a key for all possible instances — GATE frequently exploits this distinction

formulas.name: Instance Evidence vs. Schema Key
formulas.latex: \text{distinct values in current instance} \not\Rightarrow \text{candidate key of schema}
formulas.plain: Distinct values in the current instance do NOT guarantee the attribute is a candidate key of the schema
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether observed distinct values in one snapshot prove a key exists

terms.means: one particular set of rows at one point in time
the structural definition, applies to all valid instances

formulas.trap: A counter-example is easy: future tuples can repeat the value, so the schema may not enforce uniqueness

formulas.name: Counting Superkeys Given a Candidate Key
formulas.latex: \text{If } K \text{ (size } k\text{) is the only candidate key of } R \text{ (}n\text{ attributes)},\quad|\text{superkeys}| = 2^{n-k}
formulas.plain: Number of superkeys = 2^(n - k), where k = size of candidate key and n = total attributes
formulas.whenToUse: GATE numerical problems asking for the maximum or exact number of superkeys

terms.means: total number of attributes in R
number of attributes in the candidate key
all possible supersets of the candidate key formed from the remaining (n-k) attributes

formulas.trap: When there are multiple candidate keys, subsets may overlap; the formula 2^(n-k) applies only when there is exactly one candidate key of size k

formulas.name: Primary Key vs. Candidate Key
formulas.latex: \text{Primary Key} \subseteq \text{Candidate Keys},\quad \text{one CK chosen as PK}
formulas.plain: Primary key is one selected candidate key; a relation can have multiple candidate keys but exactly one primary key
formulas.whenToUse: Distinguishing PK, CK, and alternate keys in schema design questions

terms.means: candidate key — any minimal superkey
primary key — the one CK selected by the designer
alternate key — CKs not chosen as PK

formulas.trap: GATE questions may list unique attributes and ask which sets qualify as candidate keys — all minimal superkeys qualify, not just the one designated as PK

formulas.name: Composite Candidate Key
formulas.latex: \text{If no single attribute is a superkey, the CK is composite: }|CK| \geq 2
formulas.plain: If no single attribute uniquely identifies rows, the candidate key contains 2 or more attributes
formulas.whenToUse: Schema with no single-attribute key — must find minimal multi-attribute superkeys

terms.means: number of attributes in the candidate key

formulas.trap: Check ALL subsets of a proposed composite key; if any subset is already a superkey, the original set is not minimal and hence not a CK

formulas.name: Referential Integrity Constraint
formulas.latex: \forall t_1 \in r_1,\; t_1[FK] \neq \text{NULL} \Rightarrow \exists\, t_2 \in r_2 : t_2[PK] = t_1[FK]
formulas.plain: Every non-NULL foreign key value in the referencing relation must match a primary key value in the referenced relation
formulas.whenToUse: Any question about whether a state of two tables satisfies referential integrity

terms.means: referencing relation (contains the foreign key)
referenced relation (contains the primary key)
foreign key attribute(s) in r_1
primary key attribute(s) in r_2

formulas.trap: NULL values in the FK column are allowed — they do NOT violate referential integrity; only non-NULL FK values must match

formulas.name: Operations That Can Violate Referential Integrity
formulas.latex: \begin{array}{ll} \text{INSERT into } r_1 & \text{may violate (new FK may not match any PK)} \\ \text{DELETE from } r_2 & \text{may violate (PK removed, FK still exists)} \\ \text{INSERT into } r_2 & \text{never violates} \\ \text{DELETE from } r_1 & \text{never violates} \end{array}
formulas.plain: INSERT into referencing table or DELETE from referenced table can violate RI. INSERT into referenced or DELETE from referencing never violates RI.
formulas.whenToUse: Questions listing database operations and asking which can potentially violate referential integrity

terms.means: referencing relation (has FK)
referenced relation (has PK)

formulas.trap: UPDATE is not listed separately but is equivalent to DELETE + INSERT; UPDATE of FK in r_1 or UPDATE of PK in r_2 can also violate RI

formulas.name: ON DELETE CASCADE
formulas.latex: \text{DELETE } t_2 \text{ from } r_2 \Rightarrow \text{auto-DELETE all } t_1 \in r_1 \text{ where } t_1[FK] = t_2[PK]
formulas.plain: When a referenced tuple is deleted, all referencing tuples with matching FK values are automatically deleted (cascades)
formulas.whenToUse: Problems with ON DELETE CASCADE where you must track which rows survive after deletions propagate

terms.means: tuple deleted from referenced relation r_2
tuple in referencing relation r_1 that gets auto-deleted

formulas.trap: Cascade can be recursive/chain if FK references the same table's PK (self-referential); deletions can propagate multiple levels

formulas.name: Self-Referential Foreign Key with Cascade
formulas.latex: R(A,C),\; C \xrightarrow{FK} A \Rightarrow \text{deleting a row may cascade to rows whose C equals the deleted A}
formulas.plain: If FK and PK are in the same table, deleting a row cascades to delete all rows where the FK column equals the deleted PK value
formulas.whenToUse: GATE problems with a single-table foreign key (C references A in the same table R) and ON DELETE CASCADE

terms.means: primary key column in R
foreign key column referencing A in the same table R

formulas.trap: The cascade chain can continue: if deleted rows themselves had other rows pointing to them, those are deleted too — trace step by step

formulas.name: ON DELETE SET NULL
formulas.latex: \text{DELETE } t_2 \text{ from } r_2 \Rightarrow t_1[FK] \leftarrow \text{NULL for all matching } t_1 \in r_1
formulas.plain: When a referenced tuple is deleted, matching FK values in the referencing table are set to NULL instead of deleting the row
formulas.whenToUse: When the schema specifies ON DELETE SET NULL and you must determine the resulting table state

terms.means: indicates absence of a value; a NULL FK does not violate referential integrity

formulas.trap: If FK is also part of the primary key of r_1 it cannot be NULL; SET NULL would then fail and the operation is rejected

formulas.name: Referential Integrity as Subset Condition
formulas.plain: The set of non-NULL FK values in r1 must be a subset of the PK values in r2
formulas.whenToUse: Formally verifying whether two given table instances satisfy referential integrity

terms.means: projection of r_1 onto foreign key column(s)
projection of r_2 onto primary key column(s)

formulas.trap: Projection must match on the exact attribute domain; if FK maps to a non-primary unique key in r_2, standard RI requires it to reference the primary key

formulas.name: Entity Integrity Constraint
formulas.latex: \text{Primary key attributes must be } \textbf{NOT NULL} \text{ and } \textbf{UNIQUE}
formulas.plain: No primary key column may contain NULL, and the PK value must be unique across all tuples
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether a schema or instance violates the entity integrity rule (not just referential integrity)

terms.means: PK column cannot hold a null value
no two tuples can have the same PK value

formulas.trap: Referential integrity and entity integrity are separate constraints; GATE may ask about both in the same question — do not conflate them
